summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes tech companies for using meaningless corporate jargon on their websites instead of clearly explaining what their products do. He singles out KnowNow and GrandCentral as examples of companies whose homepages are completely inscrutable, even to a veteran software designer. He argues that if he can't understand what a product does after twenty years in software, the companies are failing at basic communication and hurting their own products.
key_points: Tech company websites frequently use vague, buzzword-laden descriptions that fail to communicate what their products actually do
If your target audience (experienced software professionals) can't understand your product description, you have a communication problem that's hurting sales
Corporate jargon like 'Internet-scale event routing solutions' and 'enabling the real-time enterprise' are meaningless to the people making purchase decisions
Companies inflate simple facts with unnecessary complexity — 'four main categories of functionality' instead of just 'four features'
money_quotes: When I go to a company's web site and it says that their product 'provides Internet-scale event routing solutions that seamlessly integrate information among Web services, applications and users,' I want to barf.
I'm a software designer! I'm your target audience, and I can't make head or tail of it!
I have a feeling that both of these products are interesting and useful. But listen, bubbie: if I can't understand it, and I've been writing software for twenty years, then I don't know who will.
You're ruining your products because you can't explain in two sentences what they do in a way which would be meaningful to the people who are going to make the purchase decision.
I think that means 'Grand Central has four features.' Maybe somebody thought that it didn't sound cool enough that way.
key_insight: If you can't explain your product in plain language that your target audience understands, your marketing is actively sabotaging your product.
